elements.sent-2411-001: We consider the Lane--Emden equation with a supercritical nonlinearity with an inhomogeneous Dirichlet boundary condition on an infinite cone.
elements.sent-2411-002: Under suitable conditions for the boundary data and the exponent of nonlinearity, we give a complete classification of the existence/nonexistence of a solution with respect to the size of boundary data.
elements.sent-2411-003: Moreover, we give a result on the multiple existence of solutions via bifurcation theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also state results on Hardy--H\'{e non equations on infinite cones as a generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper concerns a structure of solutions to a boundary value problem of the Lane--Emden equation \begin{equation}\label{L}\tag{$L_\kappa$} \left\{\begin{aligned} -\Delta u =u^p\quad in\quad \Omega, u >0\quad in\quad \Omega, u =\kappa\mu on\quad \partial\Omega\setminus\{0\}, \end{aligned}\right.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-006: Here, $\Omega$ is an infinite cone in $\R^N$, namely \begin{equation}\label{eq:cone} \Omega:=\{r\theta\in\R^N:r>0,\theta\in A\}\end{equation} with $N\ge 2$ and $A$ being a subdomain of the unit sphere $S^{N-1}$ with a smooth boundary.
elements.sent-2411-007: A typical example of an infinite cone is the half-space $\R^N_+:=\{(x_1,\ldots,x_N)\in\R^N: x_N>0\}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, under appropriate assumptions on the exponent of the nonlinearity $p$ and the boundary data $\mu$, we prove the existence/nonexistence and multiplicity of solutions to problem \eqref{L .}
elements.sent-2411-009: More precisely, our results state the existence of two constants $0\le\kappa_*<\kappa^*<\infty$ with the following properties.
elements.sent-2411-010: If $0<\kappa<\kappa^*$, then problem \eqref{L possesses a solution.}
elements.sent-2411-011: If $\kappa>\kappa^*$, then problem \eqref{L possesses no solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-012: If $\kappa=\kappa^*$, then problem \eqref{L possesses a unique solution.}
elements.sent-2411-013: If $\kappa_*<\kappa<\kappa^*$, then problem \eqref{L possesses at least two solutions.}
